did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning method, specifically a faster region-based convolutional neural network (Faster R-CNN), to classify clinical images of pigmented skin lesions into six classes (malignant melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, senile lentigo, and hematoma/hemangioma) and to perform two-class classification (benign vs. malignant). The AI model's performance was evaluated and compared against board-certified dermatologists and trainees.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a deep learning system (FRCNN) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods including patient data extraction (5846 images from 3551 patients), model training, and comparative performance evaluation against dermatologists, reporting new quantitative results such as accuracy rates and p-values.

title: The Development of a Skin Cancer Classification System for Pigmented Skin Lesions Using Deep Learning
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning system using a faster region-based convolutional neural network (FRCNN) to classify clinical images of pigmented skin lesions into six classes and distinguish between benign and malignant tumors. The model was trained on 4,732 clinical images from 2,885 patients and evaluated on a test set of 200 images randomly sampled from 666 patients. The system's performance was compared against 10 board-certified dermatologists and 10 dermatologic trainees who performed the same classification tasks. For six-class classification, the FRCNN achieved an accuracy of 86.2%, significantly outperforming dermatologists (79.5%) and trainees (75.1%). In two-class classification (benign vs. malignant), the FRCNN also demonstrated superior accuracy (91.5%) compared to dermatologists (86.6%) and trainees (85.3%), along with lower false positive rates.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant tumor class; 1611 images from 710 patients in total dataset; 136 images in test set.
Malignant tumor class; 401 images from 270 patients in total dataset; 44 images in test set.
Benign tumor class; 2837 images from 1839 patients in total dataset; 349 images in test set.
Benign tumor class; 746 images from 555 patients in total dataset; 96 images in test set.
Benign tumor class; 79 images from 65 patients in total dataset; 15 images in test set.
Benign tumor class; 172 images from 147 patients in total dataset; 26 images in test set.

all_task_types.target: Six-class classification of pigmented skin lesions (malignant melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, senile lentigo, and hematoma/hemangioma) and two-class classification (benign vs. malignant).
all_task_types.details: The model performs discrete diagnostic category prediction. It classifies images into one of six specific lesion classes or into a binary benign/malignant category. The paper reports accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, false positive rates, and positive predictive values for these classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: National Cancer Center Hospital Clinical Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of clinical images of brown to black pigmented skin lesions extracted from the Department of Dermatologic Oncology at the National Cancer Center Hospital, Tokyo, Japan, collected between 2001 and 2017.

use.training: Used to train the Faster R-CNN model with bounding-box annotations; images of BCC, SL, and H/H were twice oversampled.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance; 666 images (one per patient) were randomly selected from 3551 patients. A subset of 200 images was randomly sampled to create 10 test patterns for comparison with dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the accuracy of two-class and six-class classification between the FRCNN model and dermatologists (BCDs) or trainees (TRNs).
To determine statistical significance for the comparisons made using the paired t-test.
To report the variability of accuracy scores for the FRCNN, BCDs, and TRNs in both six-class and two-class classifications.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in classification accuracy between the FRCNN model and the dermatologists (BCDs) or trainees (TRNs).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in classification accuracy between the FRCNN model and the dermatologists (BCDs) or trainees (TRNs).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P-values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant. The FRCNN's accuracy was significantly higher than that of BCDs (p = 0.0081 for six-class, p = 0.0083 for two-class) and TRNs (p < 0.00001 for six-class, p < 0.001 for two-class). The accuracy of BCDs was not significantly higher than that of TRNs (p = 0.070 for six-class, p = 0.40 for two-class).

National Cancer Center Hospital Clinical.description: Test set consisting of 666 images from 666 patients, evaluated via 10 test patterns of 200 images each (total 2000 evaluations per group).

six_class_classification.notes: Except for test #2, FRCNN had higher accuracy than dermatologists. Only one out of 20 dermatologists had higher accuracy than FRCNN in six-class classification.

two_class_classification.notes: FRCNN achieved highest accuracy and sensitivity. BCDs achieved highest specificity. False negative rates were similar across groups (FRCNN: 16.7%, BCDs: 13.7%, TRNs: 16.5%).

architecture_details.description: Merges Region Proposal Network (RPN) and Fast R-CNN into a unified network by sharing convolutional features with 'attention' mechanisms.

training_details.learning_rate: 1 × 10^-3 (decreased by a factor of 10 after 40 and 80 epochs)
training_details.oversampling: Images of BCC, SL, and H/H were twice oversampled during training

classification_or_output_head.test_time_augmentation: Horizontal flip (2 patterns), 72 degree rotations (5 patterns), 1x/1.2x/1.4x zoom (3 patterns), yielding 30 patterns total; final prediction selected from highest confidence among all predictions

preprocessing_techniques: Bounding-box annotation of lesions in training images
Quality control to ensure images were of sufficient quality for dermatologist diagnosis
Data augmentation during training: horizontal flip, random distort, 90 and 180 degree rotations, random cropping, and zoom
Oversampling of images for specific classes (BCC, SL, H/H) during training
Test-time augmentation during inference: horizontal flip (2 patterns), 72 degree rotations (5 patterns), and 1x, 1.2x, or 1.4x zoom (3 patterns)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal flip, random distort, 90 and 180 degree rotations, random cropping, and zoom were applied to the training images.
Images of basal cell carcinoma (BCC), senile lentigo (SL), and hematoma/hemangioma (H/H) were twice oversampled during training.

During inference, input images underwent transformations of horizontal flip (2 patterns), 72 degree rotations (5 patterns), and 1x, 1.2x, or 1.4x zoom (3 patterns), yielding 30 patterns total. The predicted region with the highest confidence among all predictions was selected as the final prediction.

conclusions: The deep learning system using Faster R-CNN (FRCNN) achieved a six-class classification accuracy of 86.2%, which was statistically significantly higher than that of board-certified dermatologists (79.5%, p = 0.0081) and dermatologic trainees (75.1%, p < 0.00001).

In two-class classification (benign vs. malignant), the FRCNN achieved an accuracy of 91.5%, sensitivity of 83.3%, and specificity of 94.5%, significantly outperforming board-certified dermatologists (accuracy 86.6%, p = 0.0083) and trainees (accuracy 85.3%, p < 0.001).

The FRCNN demonstrated a lower false positive rate (5.5%) compared to board-certified dermatologists (13.4%) and trainees (14.1%), resulting in a higher positive predictive value (84.7%) than both dermatologist groups (70.5% and 68.5%, respectively).

Only one out of 20 dermatologists achieved higher accuracy than the FRCNN in six-class classification, indicating superior overall performance of the AI system in this clinical image classification task.
The study concludes that the developed deep learning system has potential for implementation in society for use by the general public to improve the prognosis of skin cancer.
important_remarks: The study is limited to brown to black pigmented skin lesions, excluding erythematosus skin malignancies (e.g., mycosis fungoides, extramammary Paget’s disease) and actinic keratosis.
The dataset is derived from a single institution (National Cancer Center Hospital, Tokyo), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or clinical settings.
The system was trained and evaluated on clinical images taken with digital single-lens reflex cameras with macro lenses and flash, not dermoscopic images or standard smartphone camera images.
The test set was small (666 images from 666 patients), and performance was evaluated using 10 test patterns of 200 images each, which may not fully represent real-world variability.
The FRCNN model showed less variation in performance (lower standard deviation) compared to dermatologists, suggesting higher robustness, but this was measured in a controlled test environment.
The paper acknowledges that FRCNN has difficulty identifying objects from low-resolution images due to weak capacity to identify local texture, which is a concern for mobile phone applications.
The study did not include external validation; the model was only tested on data from the same institution where it was trained.
The goal of implementing the system for use by the general public raises concerns about the reproducibility of shooting conditions and equipment differences in real-world scenarios.
The study excluded cases that were difficult to differentiate clinically, potentially introducing selection bias towards more typical or easier-to-diagnose lesions.

The positive predictive value of the FRCNN (84.7%) was higher than that of dermatologists (70.5% for BCDs, 68.5% for TRNs), indicating fewer false positives, which is critical for reducing user anxiety.
